jokes.text: I was almost killed on the way here. I'm on the 101, and all of a sudden the entire cast of miss Saigon comes out of nowhere and tries to sideswipe me.
jokes.setup: Hank casually mentions almost being killed driving to work
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific threat of the entire Miss Saigon cast trying to sideswipe him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist specificity - why Miss Saigon specifically? The randomness feels earned through Hank's character paranoia and Hollywood setting.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I was almost killed on the way here. I'm on the 101, and all of a sudden the entire cast of miss Saigon comes out of nowhere and tries to sideswipe me.

jokes.text: These asians... I mean, they must have a deal with the dmv. They... they keep getting the licenses.
jokes.setup: Hank's Miss Saigon story leads to racial commentary
jokes.punchline: His casually racist and illogical theory about Asian drivers and the DMV
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank casual prejudice - fits character perfectly but the joke itself is predictable cringe rather than clever subversion.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: These asians... I mean, they must have a deal with the dmv. They... they keep getting the licenses.

jokes.text: Oh, it's an aids thing, right? / Oh, you know about it? / No, but it's always an aids thing.
jokes.setup: Hank asks Larry to sponsor his charity walkathon
jokes.punchline: Larry's cynical but accurate observation that celebrity charity events are predictably AIDS-related
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry cynicism with great economy. The deadpan delivery of an uncomfortable truth about 90s celebrity charity culture.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, it's an aids thing, right?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, you know about it?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, but it's always an aids thing.

jokes.text: Well, how does a dollar a mile sound? / 20 whole dollars. Can a cure be far behind?
jokes.setup: Hank asks for sponsorship money per mile
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic calculation that $20 should surely cure AIDS
jokes.explanation: Larry's signature bitter sarcasm about charitable giving amounts. The mock-hopeful tone makes the cynicism land perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Well, how does a dollar a mile sound?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: 20 whole dollars. Can a cure be far behind?

jokes.text: Happy to. How's 20 bucks a mile? / Oh, that's great. That's $400. / What is the cause, aids? Yeah, make it 25.
jokes.setup: Larry offers generous sponsorship to second person asking
jokes.punchline: He increases the amount when he learns it's AIDS-related, showing his guilt/virtue signaling
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency - Larry being more generous to the second person shows his performative charity instincts.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Happy to. How's 20 bucks a mile?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great. That's $400.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What is the cause, aids? Yeah, make it 25.

jokes.text: It'd be nice to get some from a woman occasionally, wouldn't it?
jokes.setup: Beverly mentions fan mail underwear from same male sender
jokes.punchline: Larry's casual wish for female underwear fan mail reveals his creepy side
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry inappropriateness - the casual way he expresses this creepy thought is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It'd be nice to get some from a woman occasionally, wouldn't it?

jokes.text: A few seconds ago, I saw Meg Ryan's head floating above my credenza.
jokes.setup: Artie explains he's high from paint fumes
jokes.punchline: Specific hallucination of Meg Ryan's disembodied head in his office
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Meg Ryan and 'credenza' makes this absurdist image wonderfully vivid. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: A few seconds ago, I saw Meg Ryan's head floating above my credenza.

jokes.text: No, my... my interior designer's named bryn mawr. / Oh, uh, no, Bob told me he makes a lot more money using that name.
jokes.setup: Confusion over designer's name
jokes.punchline: Interior designer uses pretentious fake name 'Bryn Mawr' to charge more money
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of LA pretension - using an Ivy League college name as a fake identity to justify higher prices.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, my... my interior designer's named bryn mawr.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Oh, uh, no, Bob told me he makes a lot more money using that name.

jokes.text: Well, he said this one is from portugal, so it'll cost you 6,000 extra. / Heh. Iordy, I did some grand humping in portugal. I got a mysterious itch in lisbon.
jokes.setup: Discussion of expensive Portuguese tiles
jokes.punchline: Artie's inappropriate oversharing about his sexual adventures and STDs in Portugal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - the paint fumes give him excuse for oversharing. 'Grand humping' and 'mysterious itch' are perfectly crude.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Well, he said this one is from portugal, so it'll cost you 6,000 extra.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Heh. Iordy, I did some grand humping in portugal. I got a mysterious itch in lisbon.

jokes.text: You know, it's getting more and more difficult to pretend you're not here.
jokes.setup: Artie keeps interrupting despite asking to be ignored
jokes.punchline: Larry's dry acknowledgment that Artie's presence is impossible to ignore
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan delivery of the obvious - Larry's polite way of telling Artie to shut up.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know, it's getting more and more difficult to pretend you're not here.

jokes.text: Well, let's go with the one from portugal. Because, you know, if I get the cheaper one, then I'm always gonna think there's a better tile in portugal.
jokes.setup: Larry can't tell difference between identical tiles
jokes.punchline: His neurotic logic for choosing the expensive one to avoid regret
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry neurosis - the absurd psychology of luxury purchasing. Very relatable wealthy person anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, let's go with the one from portugal. Because, you know, if I get the cheaper one, then I'm always gonna think there's a better tile in portugal.

jokes.text: I don't know what you've heard about us, Mr... bobo. / Mr. Bobo. / No, just bobo.
jokes.setup: Formal job interview setting
jokes.punchline: Clown applicant's name correction - he's just 'Bobo', not 'Mr. Bobo'
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective - the formality clash between professional interview and clown persona.

dialogue.line: I don't know what you've heard about us, Mr... bobo.

dialogue.speaker: Bobo
dialogue.line: Mr. Bobo.

dialogue.speaker: Bobo
dialogue.line: No, just bobo.

jokes.text: All right, I can see what's going on. You're one of those clowns who's always on.
jokes.setup: Clown continues performing during interview
jokes.punchline: Interviewer's exasperated professional assessment of method clowning
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about professional vs. method acting approach to clowning. Mildly clever workplace satire.

dialogue.line: All right, I can see what's going on. You're one of those clowns who's always on.

jokes.text: [Water spraying sound] Oh! There you go. Jesus Christ.
jokes.setup: Clown offers to get interviewer a drink
jokes.punchline: Clown sprays interviewer with water from flower, classic clown gag
jokes.explanation: Classic slapstick but predictable. The setup is obvious and the payoff is exactly what you'd expect from a clown interview.

dialogue.line: [Water spraying sound] Oh! There you go. Jesus Christ.

jokes.text: Is it me, or does this suck? / I mean, I hate this sketch. / Yeah. So do I.
jokes.setup: Characters are performing in a sketch
jokes.punchline: They break character to complain about how bad the sketch is
jokes.explanation: Great meta-comedy - characters acknowledging their own show's weak material while performing it. Very Larry Sanders.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Is it me, or does this suck?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I mean, I hate this sketch.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah. So do I.

jokes.text: You must get laid a lot, huh? / It's the shoe thing, you know? The kick size, dick size. / Yeah, these are bruno maglis size 112.
jokes.setup: Inappropriate question to the clown about his sex life
jokes.punchline: Clown's matter-of-fact response about shoe/penis correlation with absurdly large shoe size
jokes.explanation: The absurdly specific Bruno Magli reference and size 112 elevates what could be a cheap sex joke into something more surreal.

dialogue.line: You must get laid a lot, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Bobo
dialogue.line: It's the shoe thing, you know? The kick size, dick size.

dialogue.speaker: Bobo
dialogue.line: Yeah, these are bruno maglis size 112.

jokes.text: How much, Frank? / Uh, a double, I guess, please. / I mean, how much is left?
jokes.setup: Larry asks 'how much' after Frank requests a drink
jokes.punchline: Frank thinks he's asking about drink size when Larry means 'how much money is left'
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with great economy. The misunderstanding reveals both characters' priorities perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How much, Frank?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Uh, a double, I guess, please.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I mean, how much is left?

jokes.text: I told you that it would like this in the fourth quarter. / No, you didn't. / Yeah, I did. L... when we were at the Ivy... / no. / I warned you when you were... / yes, when you were tipping the valet. / No, you didn't.
jokes.setup: Frank claims he warned Larry about financial problems
jokes.punchline: The escalating back-and-forth with Frank claiming increasingly specific (and unlikely) moments
jokes.explanation: Good cringe escalation showing Frank's desperate attempts to retroactively justify his actions.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I told you that it would like this in the fourth quarter.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, you didn't.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Yeah, I did. L... when we were at the Ivy...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: no.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I warned you when you were...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: yes, when you were tipping the valet.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: No, you didn't.

jokes.text: I don't drink. / Well, you just asked me for a drink. / I don't think that's the case. / You just asked me for a drink.
jokes.setup: Frank denies drinking problem after requesting alcohol
jokes.punchline: Frank's complete denial of something that just happened moments ago
jokes.explanation: Perfect absurdist denial - Frank's gaslighting is so immediate and blatant it becomes surreal rather than realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I don't drink.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, you just asked me for a drink.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I don't think that's the case.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You just asked me for a drink.

jokes.text: Are you an idiot? Answer me. Answer me, you stupid shit. Are you an idiot? / Get the fuck out of the office!
jokes.setup: Larry's anger building throughout the conversation
jokes.punchline: Larry's explosive repetitive questioning and profane meltdown
jokes.explanation: Good character explosion showing Larry's breaking point. The repetitive questioning adds intensity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Are you an idiot? Answer me. Answer me, you stupid shit. Are you an idiot? / Get the fuck out of the office!

jokes.text: What the fuck's the matter with him today?
jokes.setup: Frank has just been screamed at and fired for losing Larry's money
jokes.punchline: Frank's complete obliviousness to why Larry might be upset
jokes.explanation: Perfect clueless reaction. The complete disconnect between Frank's actions and his confusion is beautifully written.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: What the fuck's the matter with him today?

jokes.text: It's been over 3 hours, and the man hasn't moved a muscle. Something tells me we won't be doing a show tonight.
jokes.setup: Larry is catatonic under a pillow after his breakdown
jokes.punchline: Hank's understated assessment that they might not do the show
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan delivery of the obvious. Hank's matter-of-fact assessment of Larry's complete breakdown.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's been over 3 hours, and the man hasn't moved a muscle. Something tells me we won't be doing a show tonight.

jokes.text: Between my fucking ex-wife and the... and the ponies, I'm lucky if I can swing postage.
jokes.setup: Hank offers Larry financial help
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's broke due to alimony and gambling addiction
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation. 'Swing postage' is a nice specific detail showing how broke Hank really is.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Between my fucking ex-wife and the... and the ponies, I'm lucky if I can swing postage.

jokes.text: You're gonna be wiping your ass with $100 bills.
jokes.setup: Hank tries to cheer Larry up about future earning potential
jokes.punchline: His crude metaphor for wealth involving toilet paper money
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank vulgarity trying to be encouraging. The crudeness fits character but isn't particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You're gonna be wiping your ass with $100 bills.

jokes.text: You fucker. Don't ever do that to me again.
jokes.setup: Hank forces a hug on the depressed Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry's angry but resigned response to the unwanted physical comfort
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's discomfort with physical affection, even when he needs it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You fucker. Don't ever do that to me again.

jokes.text: I'll go over there and bounce that little fucker out the window. / What? Ha ha. Just kidding. / You know, but I do know a couple of guys who can be helpful... one quick phone call.
jokes.setup: Artie offers to help deal with Frank
jokes.punchline: Escalates from violence to claiming it's a joke to implying mob connections
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - the quick backtrack followed by doubling down on mob connections. Great dark comedy escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I'll go over there and bounce that little fucker out the window.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: What? Ha ha. Just kidding.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know, but I do know a couple of guys who can be helpful... one quick phone call.

jokes.text: I fired my accountant this week because evidently, he invested my money in a company that had plans to install tampon dispensers at the citadel.
jokes.setup: Larry doing stand-up about his financial problems
jokes.punchline: The absurdly specific and doomed investment in tampons at a military academy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist specificity. The Citadel detail makes this wonderfully specific and quotable. High-quality stand-up material.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I fired my accountant this week because evidently, he invested my money in a company that had plans to install tampon dispensers at the citadel.

jokes.text: I just found out my accountant has a 3-tiered system, actually, win, place, and show.
jokes.setup: Larry talking about his gambling accountant
jokes.punchline: Accountant uses horse racing betting terms as investment strategy
jokes.explanation: Solid wordplay connecting gambling and accounting. Professional stand-up quality with good setup/payoff structure.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I just found out my accountant has a 3-tiered system, actually, win, place, and show.

jokes.text: And about the walkathon, you don't have to feel obligated to... / Paula, come on. I'm not destitute, for God's sake.
jokes.setup: Paula tries to let Larry off the hook for charity pledge
jokes.punchline: Larry's pride won't let him admit he's broke, even when offered an out
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - Larry's ego won't let him take the easy way out even when broke.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: And about the walkathon, you don't have to feel obligated to...

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Paula, come on. I'm not destitute, for God's sake.

jokes.text: How much was it? / I think it was 25 doll... you know what? It was $10 a mile. / That's fine. / It was $5.00 a mile. / If it's $5.00 a mile, that's fine.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to remember his charity pledge amount
jokes.punchline: Paula keeps lowering the amount and Larry keeps accepting, showing his desperation
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation showing Larry's financial desperation through decreasing charity amounts. Great cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: How much was it?

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: I think it was 25 doll... you know what? It was $10 a mile.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That's fine.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: It was $5.00 a mile.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: If it's $5.00 a mile, that's fine.

jokes.text: That would be your uncle? / Yeah. That's right. Ah. Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Phil mentions his dad's brother went through same financial problems
jokes.punchline: Larry's dry clarification of basic family relationships
jokes.explanation: Mild Larry sarcasm pointing out Phil's redundant explanation. Simple but effective character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: That would be your uncle?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Yeah. That's right. Ah. Mm-hmm.

jokes.text: Yeah. Listen, this doesn't have a happy ending, does it, Phil? / No. I'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Phil starts telling a story about his uncle's financial ruin
jokes.punchline: Larry cuts to the chase, knowing Phil's 'cheering up' story will be depressing
jokes.explanation: Great Larry cynicism - he knows exactly where Phil's 'helpful' story is heading and cuts to the depressing end.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Yeah. Listen, this doesn't have a happy ending, does it, Phil?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: No. I'm sorry.

jokes.text: God, I suck at cheering people up.
jokes.setup: Phil's attempt to cheer Larry up with uncle story fails
jokes.punchline: Phil's honest self-assessment of his terrible bedside manner
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective self-aware moment. Phil's honest admission after his failed attempt at comfort.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: God, I suck at cheering people up.

jokes.text: We got him back for you. We nailed him. The two us, Artie and me. That little cocksucker Frank is down. It was beautiful.
jokes.setup: Hank excitedly reports taking revenge on Frank
jokes.punchline: His over-the-top excitement about their 'success' in getting revenge
jokes.explanation: Good setup for the coming revelation. Hank's enthusiasm contrasts with the disaster about to be revealed.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: We got him back for you. We nailed him. The two us, Artie and me. That little cocksucker Frank is down. It was beautiful.

jokes.text: It's a trellis. It's a trellis. / And this trellis... this is a gorgeous trellis. It's just... just gorgeous. And we stood there. We admired it for quite a while. Then I burned it down.
jokes.setup: Artie describes the object they targeted for revenge
jokes.punchline: The buildup of admiring something beautiful before destroying it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist buildup - the extended appreciation of beauty before casual destruction. Perfect Artie logic.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It's a trellis. It's a trellis. / And this trellis... this is a gorgeous trellis. It's just... just gorgeous. And we stood there. We admired it for quite a while. Then I burned it down.

jokes.text: Ha! Not now there ain't.
jokes.setup: Larry says there's no trellis at Frank's house
jokes.punchline: Hank's proud confirmation that they destroyed it completely
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic timing and delivery. Hank's pride in destruction before realizing they hit wrong house.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Ha! Not now there ain't.

jokes.text: There's no trellis at his new house. There's a trellis at his old house... not at his new house... the one on Benedict canyon that he just moved into.
jokes.setup: Larry realizes what happened
jokes.punchline: The dawning realization that they burned down an innocent person's property
jokes.explanation: Great cringe escalation as the truth dawns. Larry's careful explanation makes the disaster clear.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: There's no trellis at his new house. There's a trellis at his old house... not at his new house... the one on Benedict canyon that he just moved into.

jokes.text: You know, when that asian woman came out of the house screaming, I just thought she was the housekeeper.
jokes.setup: Revelation that they burned wrong house
jokes.punchline: Hank's casually racist assumption explains why they didn't realize their mistake
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank character consistency - his casual racism led to not recognizing they had wrong house.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You know, when that asian woman came out of the house screaming, I just thought she was the housekeeper.

jokes.text: 9 shows a night, every half hour. You know, it's like vaudeville days.
jokes.setup: Suggesting Vegas shows for Larry
jokes.punchline: Absurdly frequent show schedule compared to vaudeville
jokes.explanation: Good agent over-enthusiasm. The vaudeville comparison works but the math makes it absurd in a good way.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: 9 shows a night, every half hour. You know, it's like vaudeville days.

jokes.text: Is the car gonna be a compact, also?
jokes.setup: Coffee table representing luxury car in rehearsal
jokes.punchline: Larry's sarcastic question about the small coffee table representing a luxury vehicle
jokes.explanation: Good Larry sarcasm about the absurd rehearsal setup. Sharp observation about size mismatch.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Is the car gonna be a compact, also?

jokes.text: I like it. / I like it. Hot in the summer. Heh.
jokes.setup: Executive wants Larry to love the leather interior
jokes.punchline: Larry's practical complaint about leather being hot undermines the luxury appeal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry - his practical mindset clashes with advertising fantasy. The deadpan delivery sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I like it. / I like it. Hot in the summer. Heh.

jokes.text: I got a drinking problem. For the last 2 years, I've been off and on antidepressants. I hate my life, Larry.
jokes.setup: Frank arrives late at night to apologize
jokes.punchline: His oversharing of personal problems as an excuse
jokes.explanation: Frank's desperate oversharing fits character. The TMI confessional is appropriately uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: I got a drinking problem. For the last 2 years, I've been off and on antidepressants. I hate my life, Larry.

jokes.text: So this is 5% of what I lost? / Well, more or less. / Where's the rest? / Well, I'm working on it, man.
jokes.setup: Frank returns some money to Larry
jokes.punchline: The pathetically small amount and Frank's vague promise about the rest
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the inadequacy of Frank's 'solution' to Larry's financial ruin.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: So this is 5% of what I lost?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Well, more or less.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Where's the rest?

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Well, I'm working on it, man.

jokes.text: But for now... we're square, right? / What do you mean, we're square, Frank?
jokes.setup: Frank returns tiny fraction of money he lost
jokes.punchline: His delusional belief that 5% repayment makes them 'square'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank delusion - his complete disconnect from the severity of what he's done.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: But for now... we're square, right?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What do you mean, we're square, Frank?

jokes.text: These are the chins. Uh, edward and allison. And edward's mother, whose name I'm not gonna even try to pronounce.
jokes.setup: Beverly introduces the Asian family whose house was vandalized
jokes.punchline: Her casual racism in refusing to pronounce the mother's name
jokes.explanation: Typical cringe comedy racism from show business character. Fits the show's satirical tone.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: These are the chins. Uh, edward and allison. And edward's mother, whose name I'm not gonna even try to pronounce.

jokes.text: I'm so sorry about the Margaret cho show.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to be nice to Asian family
jokes.punchline: His misguided attempt to connect by referencing failed Asian-American sitcom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry foot-in-mouth moment. The specific TV reference makes his awkward attempt at connection brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry about the Margaret cho show.

jokes.text: Then let's go with the cheaper one because, uh, why waste the money?
jokes.setup: Larry again choosing between identical tiles
jokes.punchline: Complete reversal of his earlier neurotic logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback showing how financial crisis changed Larry's priorities. The complete reversal of his earlier neurotic logic is expertly crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Then let's go with the cheaper one because, uh, why waste the money?

jokes.text: Apparently, some nut drove up on the sidewalk and ran over his foot.
jokes.setup: Frank is in the hospital
jokes.punchline: The 'random' accident that injured Frank after he suspected Larry's involvement in the trellis burning
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy payoff suggesting Artie's 'guys' got Frank after all. The casual delivery makes it perfect.

dialogue.line: Apparently, some nut drove up on the sidewalk and ran over his foot.
